topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was from Mark. My stomach immediately tightened, a familiar knot forming beneath my ribs. I scrolled down, my thumb hovering over the 'Accept' button as if it were a live wire. Seeing his name, a phantom echo of scraped knees and whispered taunts from elementary school, made my palms instantly slick. I reread the request three times. Why now? What had changed? A wave of self-reproach washed over me, heavy and cold. I quickly minimized the message, turning toward the window, the glass reflecting a stranger with eyes that seemed to hold all the awkwardness of my past. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to delete the entire application, to erase the possibility of this connection before it could start.

[story 2]

When the alert popped up, my breath hitched. It was him. The boy who used to find reasons to make me feel small. A tremor ran through my hands, and I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. I stared at the profile picture—a smirk that I remembered with painful clarity—and a profound sense of unease settled deep in my chest. Every muscle in my body seemed to tense up, preparing for a confrontation I desperately wanted to avoid. *What am I supposed to do?* The thought echoed loudly in the silence of my room. I paced three steps to the bookshelf, running a hand through my hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. I couldn't bring myself to respond, paralyzed by the weight of everything that had happened between us.

[story 3]

The phone buzzed. I picked it up slowly, the sound amplified in the stillness of the apartment. It was Mark. My heart started hammering against my sternum, a frantic rhythm that made my throat feel dry. As I read the simple request, a flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor that betrayed my composure. It felt wrong, this pull toward familiarity, toward someone who represented such a sharp, unpleasant chapter of my youth. I let the phone slip from my fingers, letting it clatter onto the cushion beside me. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the churning sensation in my gut to subside, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.
